title: the AI and robotics buildout has a physical supply problem
author: IndustriousMadman
selftext:
Most conversations about future technology are about AI, robots, chips, cloud systems, automation and energy storage. But all of that still has a physical supply chain behind it.

Data centers need power cables, transformers, cooling systems and grid upgrades. Robots need motors, wiring, sensors, batteries and connectors. EVs, renewable energy, defense systems and industrial automation all pull from the same materials stack.

Copper is one of the clearest examples. It shows up almost everywhere in electrification and power delivery. If AI and robotics keep scaling, the demand side gets easier to understand. The harder question is where the new supply comes from.

I think exploration is becoming more relevant to the future-tech conversation. Before copper reaches a data center, a robot factory or a grid project, someone has to find the deposit, test the ground, run geophysics, permit the work and drill it. One small Canadian example is NovaRed Mining’s Wilmac copper-gold project in British Columbia. It covers about 16,078 hectares in the Quesnel porphyry belt near the Copper Mountain / Princeton area. It is still early-stage, but it shows what the front end of future supply looks like: land, targets, soil work, geophysics and years of field programs before anything becomes usable metal.

Future tech is usually discussed like software moves first and the physical world follows. But the physical layer might become one of the hardest parts to scale.
